title: Continued Pretraining
lesson:
Continued pretraining (often shortened to CPT, and sometimes called domain-adaptive pretraining) means taking a model that has already been trained on the open internet and feeding it a fresh mountain of raw text from your specific world, so it soaks up new knowledge and vocabulary. The word continued is the whole point: you are not starting from scratch, you are resuming the original learning process where the model makers left off.

The one big distinction to lock in first

This is the easiest topic to confuse with fine-tuning, so let us nail the difference up front.

  • Regular SFT teaches a model how to behave using prompt-and-answer pairs ("when asked this, reply like that").
  • Continued pretraining teaches a model what it knows using plain documents with no questions attached.

CPT uses the exact same training objective the model was born with: next-token prediction — read some text, guess the next word, check, adjust, repeat. (A token is a chunk of text, often a word or word-piece; see Tokens.) You just point that same machine at new reading material.

Two everyday analogies

First, think of a brilliant general doctor who trained for years on everything. You hire her for a rare-disease clinic. Before she sees patients, you have her spend a month reading every paper, case file, and textbook in that narrow field. She is not memorising scripted answers yet — she is absorbing the language, the patterns, the jargon. That month of reading is continued pretraining. The later step where she practises actual patient conversations is fine-tuning.

Second, picture a chef who already knows global cooking. You want them running a Japanese kaiseki restaurant. You do not hand them a list of "if a customer says X, plate Y." Instead you have them live in Japan for six months, eating and reading and cooking, until the ingredients, seasons, and techniques are second nature. CPT is that immersion. The base model was educated broadly; CPT makes it fluent in your domain — legal contracts, medical notes, your company's codebase, a low-resource language, or finance filings.

When you actually need it

CPT is the heavy machinery you reach for when fine-tuning alone keeps falling short. Classic signals:

  • The model constantly fumbles your domain's vocabulary.
  • You have a huge pile of unlabelled text (millions of words) but few clean question-answer pairs.
  • You are adapting to a language or symbol system the base model barely saw.

A common 2026 pattern is CPT to inject the raw knowledge, then SFT to teach response style, then a preference step like DPO to polish behaviour. Order matters: knowledge first, manners second.

What the data and training look like

The dataset is gloriously simple — no labels, no chat formatting, just clean text:

{"text": "Section 4.2. The indemnifying party shall hold harmless..."}
{"text": "Pursuant to Clause 11, force majeure events include..."}

Each line is one document (or a slice of one). The model reads it all and predicts the next token over and over. A tiny conceptual loop:

# Same objective the model was first trained with
for batch in domain_corpus:        # plain text, no Q&A
    logits = model(batch.input_ids) # raw next-word scores
    loss = next_token_loss(logits, batch.labels)
    loss.backward(); optimizer.step()

You rarely train every weight on a hobby budget. Most teams do CPT with LoRA or QLoRA (cheap methods that nudge a small set of extra weights) on tools like Unsloth or Axolotl.

⚠️ The big watch-out is catastrophic forgetting: train too hard on narrow text and the model gets amnesia about everything else — it becomes a legal savant that can no longer write a friendly email. The fix is to keep a slice of broad general text mixed in (a "replay" of its old diet, often 5–20% of the mix) and to use a gentle learning rate (how big each adjustment step is).

CPT vs SFT at a glance

Continued PretrainingSupervised Fine-Tuning
Goal: add knowledge & vocabularyGoal: shape behaviour & format
Data: raw documents, no labelsData: prompt → ideal answer pairs
Often millions of wordsOften hundreds to tens of thousands of pairs
Objective: next-token predictionObjective: copy the assistant answer
Output: a smarter base, not yet chattyOutput: a helpful assistant

One honest caveat: CPT does not guarantee the model recites facts on demand. It builds intuition and fluency, not a perfect database. For exact, citable, or fast-changing facts, retrieval (RAG) is usually cheaper and more reliable than baking knowledge into weights. Many strong products do both: CPT for fluency, RAG for precision.

💡 Rule of thumb: if you can't write good Q&A pairs because the knowledge isn't in the base model yet, you probably need CPT first. If you can, you probably just need SFT.
mentalModel: Fine-tuning teaches a model manners; continued pretraining sends it back to school. You drop the model into a private library of your domain's documents and let it keep reading — same next-word-guessing game it was born playing — until your world's vocabulary and patterns feel native.
takeaways: CPT feeds raw documents to add knowledge, not Q&A pairs.
Same objective as original training: next-token prediction.
Mix in 5–20% general text or risk catastrophic forgetting.

diagram.caption: CPT pours raw domain text into a base model to build fluency; a later SFT step turns that smarter base into a helpful assistant.

mistakes: Confusing CPT with SFT. Continued pretraining feeds raw documents to add knowledge; SFT feeds prompt-answer pairs to shape behaviour. Using plain documents when you actually needed Q&A pairs (or vice versa) wastes a whole training run.

Catastrophic forgetting. Train too hard or too long on narrow text and the model forgets general skills — it aces contracts but can't write a normal sentence. Mix in 5–20% broad general text and keep the learning rate small.

Reaching for CPT when SFT or RAG would do. CPT is expensive and data-hungry. If you only need a new tone, format, or a handful of facts, fine-tuning or RAG is far cheaper. Don't fire the cannon at a mosquito.

Expecting CPT to make the model chatty or aligned. Raw-text training produces a smarter base model that rambles and won't follow instructions. You almost always need an SFT step afterwards to make it a usable assistant.

Feeding it dirty, duplicated text. CPT copies whatever it reads, so boilerplate, broken HTML, and repeated documents get burned in. Run real curation and cleaning first — garbage in, garbage memorised.

Assuming the model can now recite facts perfectly. CPT builds fluency and intuition, not a lookup table. For exact, current, or citable facts, pair it with retrieval rather than trusting the weights alone.
exercise:
Plan (don't necessarily run) a small continued-pretraining project for a single niche. Pick a domain you know — say, board-game rules, or your favourite sport's regulations. (1) Gather ~20 real documents and convert them into JSONL where each line is {"text": "..."} with no questions or answers. (2) Write one paragraph explaining why CPT, not plain SFT, fits this case (hint: do you have raw knowledge or labelled Q&A?). (3) List the exact percentage of general-purpose text you'd mix in to avoid catastrophic forgetting, and say why.

Stretch goal: Design the full pipeline that turns your raw-text base into a helpful Q&A assistant for that niche — name each stage in order (CPT → SFT → preference step) and write one sentence on what each stage adds and why the order can't be swapped.
solution: Hint: A valid CPT line is just {"text": "To win at chess you must checkmate the opponent's king..."} — one JSON object per line, no role or assistant fields. You choose CPT here because you have lots of raw rulebook text but no ready-made question-answer pairs, and the base model may not know this niche's jargon. For forgetting: mixing in roughly 5–20% general web text keeps the old skills alive while the new domain sinks in — too little and it forgets, too much and the domain barely lands. Full pipeline: CPT first soaks up the rules and vocabulary (knowledge); SFT then teaches it to answer questions in a helpful format (behaviour); a preference step like DPO last polishes tone and picks better answers. You can't swap the order because you can't teach good answers about knowledge the model doesn't have yet — knowledge must come before manners.
summary: Continued pretraining (CPT) resumes a model's original next-token training on a big pile of raw, unlabelled documents from your domain, soaking in new knowledge and vocabulary. Unlike SFT (which shapes behaviour with Q&A pairs), CPT shapes what the model knows. Watch for catastrophic forgetting by mixing in general text, follow it with SFT to make the model usable, and lean on RAG when you need exact facts.

keyTerms.def: Resuming a base model's original training on fresh raw text from your domain, to add knowledge and vocabulary rather than behaviour.
Another name for CPT, stressing that the goal is adapting a general model to one specific field (law, medicine, a codebase, a language).
The core training game: read some text, guess the next token, compare to the real one, and nudge the weights. CPT uses this exact objective.
A model trained only by next-token prediction on broad text. It is knowledgeable but rambly and doesn't follow instructions until fine-tuned.
When heavy training on narrow data erases a model's earlier general skills, like an expert who can no longer do everyday tasks.
Blending some broad general text (often 5–20%) into the domain data during CPT to stop the model from forgetting what it already knew.
How big each weight adjustment is during training. A small learning rate in CPT helps absorb new text without violently overwriting old knowledge.
Plain documents with no questions, answers, or tags attached — the natural diet for continued pretraining.

quiz.q: Your team has 4 million words of internal legal memos but almost no question-answer pairs, and the model keeps misusing your firm's jargon. Which step fits best first?
options: Continued pretraining on the raw memos
Supervised fine-tuning on a few hand-written Q&A pairs
Nothing — just write a longer system prompt
quiz.explain: You have a huge pile of raw, unlabelled domain text but no Q&A pairs, which is the classic signal for CPT. CPT absorbs the vocabulary and patterns before any behaviour-shaping step.

quiz.q: What training objective does continued pretraining use?
options: Copying the assistant's answer from prompt-answer pairs
Ranking a chosen answer above a rejected one
Retrieving relevant documents at query time
quiz.explain: CPT reuses the exact objective the base model was born with: read text and predict the next token. Copying answers is SFT, ranking pairs is preference tuning, and retrieval is RAG.

quiz.q: After heavy CPT on narrow contract text, your model nails legal clauses but can no longer write a friendly email. What happened, and what's the standard fix?
options: The learning rate was too low; raise it sharply
Catastrophic forgetting; mix in 5–20% general text and lower the learning rate
The dataset was too clean; add more boilerplate
It needs RAG; add a vector database
quiz.explain: Training too hard on narrow data erases earlier general skills — catastrophic forgetting. Replaying a slice of broad text plus a gentle learning rate preserves the old abilities while the domain sinks in.

quiz.q: A product needs to answer with exact, frequently-changing prices and cite the source document. Why is CPT a poor fit for that requirement?
options: CPT can't run on consumer GPUs
CPT builds fluency and intuition, not a precise, up-to-date lookup table
CPT requires labelled Q&A pairs you don't have
CPT always makes the model refuse to answer
quiz.explain: Baking facts into weights yields fluency, not a reliable, citable, current database. For exact and fast-changing facts, retrieval (RAG) is cheaper and more dependable.
